title_en: The Beginning — Creation of the Heavens and Earth
content_en:
Al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah (The Beginning and the End) by Imam Ismail ibn Kathir al-Dimashqi (701–774 AH / 1301–1373 CE) is one of the most comprehensive works of Islamic historical scholarship ever produced — an encyclopedic account of world history from the creation of the heavens and earth to the eschatological events at the end of time, organized around the prophetic and Quranic account of human history. Ibn Kathir was a major scholar of the Shafi'i legal school and a distinguished student of Ibn Taymiyyah, and his work reflects both rigorous hadith criticism and a commitment to grounding historical narrative in the best-authenticated sources.

The work opens at the very beginning: the creation of the heavens and the earth, as described in the Quran and the prophetic hadiths. Ibn Kathir draws on the Quranic account: 'Indeed, your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days' (Quran 7:54), noting immediately that these six 'days' (ayyam) may not correspond to earthly twenty-four-hour days, since the concept of the terrestrial day did not exist before the creation of the sun and earth. The scholars' understanding of 'days' in this context has varied, with the majority understanding them as actual periods of time of uncertain duration rather than precisely defined calendar days.

The Quran describes the initial state of creation as waters: 'And He it is who created the heavens and the earth in six days — and His Throne was upon water' (Quran 11:7), and the Prophet ﷺ described in a narration that the first thing Allah created was the Pen (al-Qalam), which was commanded to write everything that would occur until the Day of Judgment. Ibn Kathir reconciles these various accounts by understanding them as describing different aspects and moments of the creative process rather than contradicting each other.

The creation of the heavens — the seven heavens described in multiple Quranic verses — is presented by Ibn Kathir with careful attention to both the Quranic descriptions and the theological constraints that prevent him from speculative elaboration beyond the texts. The seven heavens are affirmed as real, created realities; their composition, dimensions, and relationship to observable astronomy are not specified in the texts, and Ibn Kathir is careful not to over-read modern astronomical knowledge into the Quranic description or vice versa.

The creation of the earth — 'And after that He spread the earth' (Quran 79:30) — is described in the Quran in several places, with details about the anchoring of the earth with mountains: 'And He cast into the earth firmly set mountains lest it should shift with you' (Quran 16:15). The Quran's description of the earth's creation and preparation for human habitation — the establishing of rivers, the growing of vegetation, the regulation of the atmosphere — is presented by Ibn Kathir as a coherent account of divine providential preparation of the world for the human being who would be created to inhabit it as Allah's vicegerent (khalifah).
